coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of Ag@CQDs composite and its electrochemiluminescence application for the highly selective and sensitive detection of chloride
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue in honor of Chinese Academician Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen for his 80th birthday
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yi-Yang
Li, Nian-Lu
Jia, Li-Ping
Ma, Rong-Na
Jia, Wen-Li
Tao, Xu-Qian
Cui, Hui
Wang, Huai-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated Ag nanoparticles (Ag@CQDs) were successfully synthesized and characterized by UV-Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence, FT-IR, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), then utilized for the sensitive detection of chloride ions (Cl−). Compared to CQDs, the Ag@CQDs showed stronger electrochemiluminescence (ECL) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8. In the presence of chloride (Cl−), a significant reduction in the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs was noticed, which was used to develop a sensitive detection method of Cl−. The ECL quenching of Ag@CQDs was utilized to establish the method, of which the mechanism might be caused by the formation of AgCl on the Ag@CQDs composite surface. The prepared Cl− sensor showed a wide linear range (0.99–450nM) and low detection limit (0.33nM). Furthermore, the prepared sensor exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity towards several ion inferences.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Carbon nanodots
Ag@CQDs composite
Chloride ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Ag@CQDs modified electrode and detection of Cl−
ECL behaviors of Ag@CQDs and feasibility of Cl− detection
Detection of Cl− ions based on quenching of ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV-Vis spectra of CQDs (a) and Ag@CQDs (b). (B) The FL spectra of CQDs (a) and Ag@CQDs (b). The inset represents a picture of the water dispersion of CQDs (left) and Ag@CQDs (right).
TEM images of CQDs (a) and Ag@CQDs (b).

ECL - Potential curves of bare GCE(a), CQDs/GCE (b), and Ag@CQDs/GCE (c), in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4) in the absence (A) and presence (B) of 0.1M K2S2O8. Scan rate: 0.2Vs−1. (C) The variation in the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs/GCE during continuous potential scans. (D) The ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs/GCE without Cl− (a) and with 200nM Cl− (b).
(A) The UV-Vis spectra of Ag@CQDs: (a) without Cl− and (b) with Cl− ions. (B) FL spectra of Ag@CQDs: (a) without Cl− and (b) with Cl− (50nM).
(A)The ECL intensity of the Ag@CQDs at different concentrations of Cl− ions. (B) The linear relationship between the ECL intensity and Cl− concentrations.
The selectivity of the ECL assay for Cl− ions.
The ECL emission and the detection mechanism of Cl−.
Comparison of different methods of Cl− detection in human serum and tap water (n =4).

head.ce:title: The synthesis of Ag@CQDs composite and its electrochemiluminescence application for the highly selective and sensitive detection of chloride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated Ag nanoparticles (Ag@CQDs) were successfully synthesized and characterized by UV-Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence, FT-IR, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), then utilized for the sensitive detection of chloride ions (Cl−). Compared to CQDs, the Ag@CQDs showed stronger electrochemiluminescence (ECL) in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8. In the presence of chloride (Cl−), a significant reduction in the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs was noticed, which was used to develop a sensitive detection method of Cl−. The ECL quenching of Ag@CQDs was utilized to establish the method, of which the mechanism might be caused by the formation of AgCl on the Ag@CQDs composite surface. The prepared Cl− sensor showed a wide linear range (0.99–450nM) and low detection limit (0.33nM). Furthermore, the prepared sensor exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity towards several ion inferences.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloride ion (Cl−) is present in mammalian cells and participate in a wide variety of cell and intracellular organelle functions, including regulation of the electrical activity and pH. It also plays a role in controlling the immunological responses, cell migration, and cell proliferation/differentiation [1]. The Cl− content in the organism is relatively stable and abnormal concentrations may induce many diseases, such as cystic fibrosis and myotonia [2,3]. Thus, it is important to develop new methodologies for clinical diagnosis and effective monitoring of toxicity for early and accurate detection of the Cl−. Currently, a number of methods are used for detection of Cl− ions, including titration [4,5], fluorescence spectroscopy [6], chromatography [7,8], chemiluminescence [9], chemosensing [10] and electrochemistry [11]. However, these methods often have some shortcomings like time-consuming analysis, complicated procedures, required large sample volumes, and/or specialized skills. Thus, it is urgent to develop new diagnosis tools for the rapid, selective, and sensitive detection of Cl− ions in real samples at the trace levels.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), the smart combination of chemiluminescence and electrochemistry, is a valuable detection method because of its simplified set-up, label-free process, and low background signal [12,13]. Compared to the conventional electrochemical and chemiluminescence techniques, the ECL method was more promising because of its high sensitivity, good controllability, and extended response range [14,15]. In recent years, a series of semiconductor nanocrystals, known as quantum dots (QDs), have been reported to have ECL emission properties [16], promising for the construction of ECL biosensors [17]. However, most of the QDs require heavy metal elements and considering the strong cytotoxicity of heavy metals in health and environment, QDs are thus not ideal candidates for fabrication of biosensors [18].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention due to their fascinating optical properties, such as photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer, chemiluminescence, and electrochemiluminescence [19–22]. As an emerging type of ECL luminophores, CQDs have several obvious advantages over QDs, including excellent chemical inertness, easy labeling, and low toxicity [23]. Furthermore, CQDs can easily be obtained through facilitated synthetic methods [24–30]. Thus, CQDs may be a good alternative for the semiconductor QDs [31].

Usually, CQDs are rarely used directly in ECL tests because the ECL intensity of CQDs is very weak. Hence, in order to expand CQDs applications, the ECL intensity of CQDs should be improved. This could be achieved by CQDs doping with other elements, such as Si and N [32–34]. However, to the best of our knowledge only a handful of applications of fabricated CQDs-based ECL biosensors have been reported [18,35], especially for Cl− sensing. For example, Ding et al. [36] synthesized an Ag@CQDs with relevant ECL intensity, which can be increased by mixing 100μMS2− with the Ag@CQDs. As a result, they established a detection method of S2− based on the changes in the ECL intensity.

Herein, a novel Ag@CQDs core/shell nanoparticles are synthesized as the ECL signal reporter for label-free detection of Cl− ions that may open new promising avenues for the detection of Cl− at lower levels.

d-Glucose was obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. and silver nitrate (AgNO3, >99.8%) from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ethylene glycol, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), sodium hydroxide, glycine (>99%) were purchased from Beijing Yinfeng Century Scientific Develop Co. Ltd. A human serum sample was collected from the People's Hospital of Dongchang and tap water from the university laboratory. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH=7.4, 0.1M) was prepared from a mixture of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. A MD44 dialysis tube (MW=3500) was used for dialysis of the CQDs and double distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

The electrochemical experiments were performed with a conventional three-electrode: glassy carbon electrode as working electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode. Cyclic voltammetry experiments were carried out with a CHI 760C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua). A MPI-ECL Analyzer (Xi'an Remex Analyse Instrument Co., Ltd) was used for measurements of the ECL intensity. The photomultiplier tube (PMT) was biased at 800V during the experiments. The surface morphologies of CQDs and Ag@CQDs composite were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100). The fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded with a LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Made in the U.K.). The UV-Vis spectra were obtained with a Lambda 750 spectrometer (Manufactured in Italy). The FT-IR spectra were recorded with NICOLET 6700 optical spectrum instrument. ISE method was carried with an MI921D electrolyte analyzer (Shenzhen Yuehua Co., Ltd).

The CQDs particles were synthesized according to the literature [35]. Briefly, d-Glucose (0.75g) was dissolved in 40ml ethylene glycol/H2O (1:1, V/V). The homogeneous solution was transferred into a 100ml autoclave after supersonic treatment for 15min and then heated for 3.5h at 185°C. Afterward, 0.22g glycine was added to the mixture followed by heat treatment for 12h at 120°C. The solution was then centrifuged at 8000rpm for 30min to remove all deposits. Finally, the resulting dark brown solution was dialyzed for 3days to remove impurity materials and yield a light brown CQDs aqueous solution.

The as-prepared CQDs are found to contain abundant carboxyl moieties, suitable for metal ion binding through ion exchange or coordination reaction [37]. The synthesis of Ag@CQDs core/shell nanoparticles was carried out according to the literature [36]. The as-prepared CQDs (10ml) were mixed with aqueous AgNO3 (2.5ml, 10mM) then NaOH solution (100μl, 1M) was added to the mixture. The reaction was kept under vigorous stirring at 37°C (keep out of the sun) for 24h to produce brown Ag@CQDs composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ag@CQDs modified electrode and detection of Cl−
ce:section.ce:para: A glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Ф=3mm) was subsequently polished with 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurry. The GCE was ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and double distilled water and dried with nitrogen. Afterward, solutions of CQDs (5μL) and Ag@CQDs (5μL) were subsequently dropped on the GCE then left to dry at room temperature. The detection of Cl− was performed in 5ml of 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8 through measurement of the ECL intensity at −1.8V.

The optical properties of CQDs (5.0mgml−1) and Ag@CQDs (5.0mgml−1) were characterized by fluorescence (FL) and UV-Vis spectroscopies. As shown in Fig. 1A, the maximum adsorption peak of CQDs appears at 260nm, which agrees well with the literature [38]. On the other hand, Ag@CQDs exhibits another absorption peak located at 430nm typical of Ag nanoparticles [18]. The FL spectra of both the CQDs and Ag@CQDs are depicted in Fig. 1B. It can be observed that the emission wavelength appears at 425nm when the excitation wavelength is 335nm. The obvious quenching effect of the transmission metals in the FL spectra indicates the formation of Ag@CQDs nanostructures [18]. The inset in Fig. 1B illustrate that the color of CQDs solution is light brown (left) and that of Ag@CQDs solution is dark brown (right). The change in the color also confirms the formation of Ag@CQDs particles.

The TEM images of the prepared CQDs and Ag@CQDs are presented in Fig. 2 , where mono-dispersed spherical shape particles of CQDs with average diameters of about 7nm can be observed in Fig. 2a. The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs reveals peaks at 3440.94cm−1 and 1384.31cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of –OH, respectively (Fig. 3 ). The peaks at 1083.95cm−1 and 1638.84cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C O and CO, respectively. The latter confirms the presence of oxygen groups on the CQDs surface, where Ag+ can easily bind to the peripheral carboxylic groups of CQDs by ion exchange or coordination reactions [37]. From the TEM image of Ag@CQDs (Fig. 2b), it could be noted that CQDs obviously surround the AgNP structure. This suggests that CQDs not only enrich the Ag ions to facilitate the reduction process but also protects the structure through a coating to ensure aggregation of the Ag nanoparticles [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors of Ag@CQDs and feasibility of Cl− detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL behaviors of CQDs (5.0mgml−1) and Ag@CQDs (5.0mgml−1) are evaluated and the results are presented in Fig. 4 for the bare GCE, CQDs/GCE, and Ag@CQDs/GCE, in 0.1M PBS (pH7.4) without (A) and with (B) 0.1M K2S2O8, respectively. As expected, all ECL intensities of the bare GCE (curve a), CQDs/GCE (curve b), and Ag@CQDs/GCE (curve c) are low when K2S2O8 is absent but significantly increase when it is present. Also, it can be seen that the ECL intensity of the bare GCE is small compared to those of both the CQDs/GCE and Ag@CQDs/GCE. On the other hand, the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs/GCE is about 9-folds higher than that of CQDs/GCE with positive shifts of the onset potential. This indicates that AgNP facilitated the electron transfer and enhanced the ECL intensity of CQDs. This could be attributed to the improved conductivity of AgNP and the compact film formed using the cross-linking method between the AgNP and CQDs [36]. The recorded elevated ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs/GCE proposes that AgNP acts as a conducting bridge between the CQDs and the electrode. Furthermore, the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs/GCE is pretty stable for the continuous potential scans (Fig. 4C). However, obvious reduction in the ECL signal of Ag@CQDs/GCE at −1.8V is observed in the presence of Cl− (200nM) (Fig. 4D), demonstrating the potential application of the expected method for Cl− determination.

To gain a better understanding of the phenomena, the ECL of Ag@CQDs and Cl− quenched ECL mechanism were investigated. The ECL mechanism of Ag@CQDs may be caused by electron transfer annihilation between an anionic quantum dot radical (R − ) and the electrogenerated SO4 −
[18]. Upon polarization with a negative potential, the Ag@CQDs particles immobilized on the electrode become reduced to Ag@CQDs−, while the coreactant S2O8 2− is reduced to the strong oxidant SO4
−. In turn, SO4
− could react with the negatively charged Ag@CQDs − through electron transfer to produce the excited state Ag@CQDs* that emits light. Scheme 1 intuitively presents the ECL emission mechanism and the possible ECL process is described in Eqs. (1)-(4):
(1)
Ag @ CQDs +
e −
→ Ag @
CQDs
− •
(2) S2O8 2 − +e− →SO4 2 − +SO4 -•
(3) Ag@CQDs-• +SO4 -• →Ag@CQDs*+SO4 2−
(4)
Ag @ CQDs * → Ag @ CQDs + hν

As shown in Fig. 4B, the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs decreased after the introduction of Cl−. It is worth noting that the surface of as-prepared Ag@CQDs possesses a small amount of Ag cations/atoms [39], and the introduction of Cl− ions would form AgCl due to the interaction between Ag cations/atoms and Cl−. The latter may encapsulate AgNP and cause a decrease in conductivity of Ag@CQDs, hence reducing the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs. Furthermore, it can be observed from the UV-Vis spectra of Fig. 5A that the surface plasmon resonance band of AgNP located at 430nm vanished when Cl− is added, indicating changes in the surface structure of Ag@CQDs. On the other hand, the FL intensity rose when the Cl− ions were added (Fig. 5B). The reason for that might be due to the formation of AgCl on the Ag@CQDs composite surface, which could weaken the Ag@CQDs bonding to rescue the FL of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cl− ions based on quenching of ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As discussed above, the detection of Cl− ions could be performed based on the quenching of the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6A, the ECL intensities of Ag@CQDs (5.0mgml−1) reduced linearly as the Cl− concentration increased, following the linear regress equation (I
0
I)/I
0
=9.99×10−3 +1.8×10−3 c (nM) and R2 =0.994. The linear range is 0.99nM to 450nM and the detection limit at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 is calculated to be 0.33nM.

In order to estimate the feasibility of the fabricated sensor, the selectivity was tested towards various interfering species. Under the optimal conditions, the response to interferences, such as Br−, I−, S2−, SCN−, NO3 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, ClO4 −, and NO2 − (each 5μM) is compared. Fig. 7 reveals that 400nM Cl− ions induce an obvious quenching to the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs while other anions generate a slight quenching effect. The concentrations of the interfering ions were 12.5-folds higher than that of the Cl− ions and when the concentrations of interfering ions decreased, the quenching effect became weak. In real samples, the concentrations of interfering ions are generally lower than 5μM. Thus, the proposed method is highly selective for the detection of Cl− ions.

It has been reported that S2− can significantly change the ECL intensity of the Ag@CQDs [36], but results from this study showed that the influence is weak. In the reference [36], different concentrations of S2− were mixed with Ag@CQDs composite to generate Ag@CQDs-Ag2S that in turn, was dropped on the GCE surface. Here, we dropped the Ag@CQDs on GCE as the working electrode and interferences like S2−, I−, Br−, SO3 2−, NO2 − are added to the PBS solution. K2S2O8 acts as a co-reactant with a concentration of 0.1M and the levels of I−, Br−, S2−, SO3 2−, NO2 − ions are far less than that of S2O8 2−. It is well known that S2O8 2− is a strong oxidant that could transform I−, Br−, S2−, SO3 2−, and NO2 − ions into I2, Br2, S, SO4 2−, NO3 −. This prevents these ions from reacting with Ag cations/atoms, inducing lower effects on the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs. However, S2O8 2− can hardly react with Cl− ions at room temperature and even under heat treatment, the reaction between S2O8 2− and Cl− ions is still very slow. As a result, Cl− can easily react with AgNP and decrease the ECL intensity of Ag@CQDs/GCE.

The practical application of the developed Ag@CQDs/GCE system was demonstrated through the detection of Cl− in tap water and human serum samples and the results are presented in Table 1 . The testing results of tap water are found in good agreement with those obtained by the potentiometric titration method. The results of human serum samples are also in accordance with those of the ISE method. This implies that the proposed method can be used for the analysis of Cl− in real samples.

A core/shell Ag@CQDs is prepared by the direct reduction of AgNO3 in CQDs solution. Compared with CQDs, the obtained Ag@CQDs exhibited much stronger ECL intensity due to the improved conductivity of AgNP. A novel ECL sensing platform for the detection of Cl− based on the ECL quenching of Ag@CQDs was developed. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed Ag@CQDs/GCE sensor showed a wide linear range (0.99–450nM) and low detection limit (0.33nM) of Cl− ions. Furthermore, the prepared sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards several interfering ions. This method would provide a promising alternative procedure for the detection of Cl−.
There is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375055, 21405070, 21505063 and 21427808).
